name: Scott Schwab
occupation: Kansas Secretary of State; former state legislator and business executive
hometown: Overland Park, Kansas (grew up in Great Bend)
education: Bachelor of Arts, Fort Hays State University, 1994
family: Wife Michele; four sons: Nathan, Caleb (deceased 2016), Alex, and Isaac
whoTheyAre:
Scott Schwab grew up in Great Bend, Kansas, graduated from Fort Hays State University in 1994, and built a career in sales and business before entering politics. He worked as an agent for the Kansas Farm Bureau, moved into pharmaceutical sales training for a Fortune 50 company, and later served as executive vice president of a durable medical equipment firm in the Kansas City area. He and his wife Michele live in Overland Park with their sons Nathan, Alex, and Isaac. Their son Caleb was killed at age 10 in 2016 on the Verruckt waterslide at Schlitterbahn Kansas City -- a tragedy that Schwab has spoken about publicly and processed through his Christian faith.

Schwab was first elected to the Kansas House in 2002, representing District 49 in Johnson County. He served seven terms over roughly twelve years, rising to Speaker Pro Tempore and chairing the Elections Committee. In 2006 he left the House to run for Congress but lost in the Republican primary, then returned to the legislature in 2009. He was elected Secretary of State in 2018, re-elected in 2022, and served as president of the National Association of Secretaries of State in 2023. In January 2025, he became the first Republican to formally enter the 2026 governor's race. In March 2025, he disclosed a Stage 1 lung cancer diagnosis, underwent surgery, and said he would make a full recovery.

Schwab identifies as a proud son of Great Bend, a Christian, and a believer in the American dream. His brother Rick is a pastor who spoke at Caleb's funeral. His father, Rick W. Schwab, passed away in July 2024.
theirRecord:
As Secretary of State since 2019, Schwab has focused on election security. He led implementation of the Kansas voter ID law (the Secure and Fair Elections Act), championed a 2022 bill banning internet-connected voting equipment, and pushed the SAVE Kansas Act to maintain accurate voter rolls. In 2025, Kansas became one of the first states to use the federal SAVE database to verify citizenship of registered voters, earning praise from President Trump. He also confronted the U.S. Postal Service after roughly 1,000 Kansas primary ballots were mishandled in 2024, calling the situation "unacceptable" and demanding answers from Postmaster General DeJoy. After the 2020 election, Schwab publicly rejected claims that the vote was rigged, saying he had "seen no evidence" of organized fraud -- a position that put him at odds with some in his own party. In 2022, he defeated a primary challenger who ran on election conspiracy theories, winning 55% to 45%.

His record also includes significant controversy. In the case Loud Light & Hammet v. Schwab, the Kansas Court of Appeals unanimously ruled in 2022 that Schwab violated the Kansas Open Records Act by directing a private vendor to disable software access to provisional ballot data, making public records effectively inaccessible. Even after the ruling, his office refused to provide the records. The ACLU of Kansas called it a "victory for government transparency." Separately, the League of Women Voters and other groups sued over two 2021 voting laws enacted under his watch that tightened ballot-collection rules and signature-matching requirements. The Kansas Supreme Court partially upheld and partially remanded those challenges. A 2020 DNC lawsuit comparing Schwab to predecessor Kris Kobach was dismissed by a district court.

In the Kansas House, Schwab chaired the Elections Committee and served on the Taxation and Commerce committees. He supported abortion restrictions, Second Amendment protections, and local control of schools. His campaign for governor centers on property tax reform, cutting regulations for businesses, banning foreign land purchases near military bases, and bringing manufacturing jobs to rural Kansas.
whatYouShouldKnow: Schwab is the sitting Kansas Secretary of State running for governor -- he already holds statewide office and has been in elected positions almost continuously since 2003.
He publicly rejected claims that the 2020 election was stolen, saying 'I have seen no evidence of that,' and defeated an election-conspiracy challenger in the 2022 Republican primary 55% to 45%.

A Kansas appeals court unanimously ruled in 2022 that Schwab violated the Kansas Open Records Act by directing a vendor to disable access to provisional ballot data. His office continued to withhold the records even after the ruling.

Schwab's 10-year-old son Caleb was killed in 2016 on the Verruckt waterslide at Schlitterbahn Kansas City. The family received a $19.7 million wrongful death settlement -- the largest for a minor in Kansas history. Schwab later helped change state law to require government inspection of amusement park rides.
His 2025 year-end campaign filing shows $1,418,671.56 raised, but $1,045,000 of that came from personal loans to his own campaign. He had about $1.05 million cash on hand.
In March 2025, Schwab disclosed a Stage 1 lung cancer diagnosis. He had surgery and said he would make a full recovery. At a January 2026 debate, observers noted he struggled with a weakened voice.
President Trump praised Schwab in 2025 for being one of the first state leaders to use the federal SAVE system to verify voter citizenship on Kansas voter rolls.

Schwab has made property tax reform the centerpiece of his campaign, criticizing both Governor Kelly and legislative leaders for delivering only a '$25.88 property tax break for the owner of a $150,000 home.'
whereTheyWorship:
Scott Schwab and his family are active members of LifeMission Church in Olathe, Kansas. The church was founded in 2002 by Pastor Clint Sprague, starting as a Bible study in his home, and is a non-denominational congregation. When Caleb was killed in 2016, Pastor Sprague served as the family's public spokesman, and both the visitation and funeral were held at LifeMission Church. Caleb had attended church with his family the morning of the day he died.

Schwab has spoken openly about how his faith carried him through the loss of his son. In a 2018 FOX4 interview, he said: 'God is real, and he's been very real to me. Palpable. To my wife and to our kids, too.' His brother Rick Schwab is a pastor who also spoke at Caleb's funeral. On his campaign website, Schwab writes: 'As a Christian, my faith has guided me at home and in my work as Secretary of State. As Governor, I will bring those values to the job everyday.'

church.name: LifeMission Church
church.details: Founded in 2002 by Pastor Clint Sprague in Olathe, Kansas. Located at 16111 S Lone Elm Rd, Olathe, KS. Mission: 'Dedicated to seeing lives and communities transformed by God's presence and purpose.'

quotes.text: God is real, and he's been very real to me. Palpable. To my wife and to our kids, too.
quotes.source: FOX4 Kansas City interview with John Holt

quotes.text: Was there organized, rigged fraud that changed the outcome of the election? I have seen no evidence of that.
There are people who try to make money off conspiracy theories -- it's nothing new.
As a Christian, my faith has guided me at home and in my work as Secretary of State. As Governor, I will bring those values to the job everyday.
When my kids were born, Nathan was my pleasure, Caleb was my joy, Alex was my delight and Isaac was my treasure.
We use a mix of laws, regulations, and internal reforms to deliver better service, protect election integrity, modernize the agency, and run government more efficiently.
quotes.source: Kansas Secretary of State press release

quotes.text: Good government isn't about how much government does. It's about making smart, practical improvements that save money and serve Kansans better.
quotes.source: Kansas Secretary of State press release

quotes.text: They call me a career politician, but no one's insulting my integrity.
It is frustration that the court hates its Legislature. That's unholy. I feel horrible that a young woman can be manipulated to have an abortion, and there's no recourse.

campaignFinance.narrative: Schwab's campaign is largely self-financed. Of the $1.42 million raised by the end of 2025, a full $1,045,000 came from personal loans to his own campaign. That leaves roughly $375,000 in outside contributions -- a real but relatively narrow donor base for a statewide race. He spent about $365,000 and had $1,053,138.50 cash on hand heading into 2026. The Kansas Reflector described Schwab and several other governor candidates as 'millionaire candidates' who 'burnish fundraising with personal loans.' Schwab's personal wealth likely stems from a combination of his business career in pharmaceutical sales and medical equipment and the $19.7 million wrongful death settlement his family received from Schlitterbahn in 2017.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: The $1,045,000 in personal loans dominates the filing. The itemized donor list is publicly available through the Kansas Ethics Commission filing but consists mostly of smaller individual contributions. No major PAC donors were identified in the reporting period.
